segments.s482-g89: پزشک خردمند را داد places the definite object (پزشک خردمند, “the wise physician”) before the verb; را is the Persian definite-object marker attached to that noun phrase. داد is the 3rd-person past of دادن (“gave”), and the direct object being given is omitted here (understood from the prior sentence). The conjunction و links this clause to گفت, where گفت is the perfect/past-tense verb “said,” introducing reported speech.
segments.s482-g90: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of what was said. با is the preposition 'with' and پاک رایت is an adjective-noun phrase 'pure standard/banner' (adjective before noun). خرد is the noun 'wisdom' and باد is the optative particle 'may' (a poetic/older Persian marker of wish) placed after the noun; جفت functions as a predicate meaning 'paired'/'become a pair' — together باد جفت expresses the optative 'may ... be paired.' The word order (subject + باد + predicate) is characteristic of literary Persian optative constructions.
segments.s482-g17: شوی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/optative of شدن used here as a wish ('may you become'). بر تن خویشتن is a prepositional phrase literally 'upon/for your body/self' meaning 'in your own person'; کامگار is an adjective (literally 'satisfied/successful') that completes the predication and follows the phrase it modifies, a common poetic word order.
segments.s482-g18: دلت is دل + the 2nd-person enclitic ت ('your heart'). گردد (گرد + -د) is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of گشتن used to mean 'becomes / will become' here. چو introduces a comparison ('as/like' or 'when') and links to the simile خرم بهار ('the joyful/lively spring'). شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy/gleeful'.
segments.s482-g19: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' modifying رنگ; here رنگ refers to complexion/appearance and is followed by چهرت (چهر + ت) 'your face'. به جای آورد is a verbal phrase meaning 'bring to its place / restore / cause to appear' with آورد as the main verb placed at the end, typical Persian verb-final order.
segments.s482-g20: به هر کار پاکیزه is a prepositional noun phrase 'in/for every pure/righteous affair' (پاکیزه qualifying کار). رای آورد is a light-verb collocation where رای ('counsel, plan') is the object and آورد ('bring/obtain') is the verb meaning 'gain/obtain (counsel or approval)'; verb-final order places آورد at the end.
segments.s482-g21: نگردد is the present/subjunctive form of گشتن with the negative prefix ن- ('may not become' / 'let not ... become'). پراگنده مویت is an adjectival phrase 'your scattered hair' (مویت = مو + possessive enclitic -ت), with adjectival modifier پراگنده preceding the noun. سپید is the complement adjective 'white'.
segments.s482-g22: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', so ز گیتی سپیدی literally 'from the world (the) whiteness' = 'the world's whiteness'. کند is the 3rd-person singular present/past-helper 'does/makes' (here 'make'), and ناامید is an adjectival result ('hopeless' / 'despairing'); the agent or patient (you) is understood but omitted, so 'let not the world's whiteness make (you) hopeless.'
segments.s482-g23: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بدو is the dative pronoun (به + او) 'to him/unto him'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech). نشنیده‌ام is the 1st-person perfect/compound negative 'I have not heard' or 'I have not heard of it' and functions as Alexander's reported remark.
segments.s482-g24: نه کس را combines the negative particle نه 'no/not' with کس 'anyone' and the object marker را (emphatic/definite object). ز شاهان 'of the kings' marks the class from which Alexander excludes someone. چنین is 'such/like this'. دیده‌ام is the 1st-person perfect 'I have seen' — together the clause means 'I have seen no one among kings like this.'
segments.s482-g49: بدو گفت is literally 'to him said' (indirect object 前置 + past verb), a common Persian order meaning 'he said to him.' کز is the archaic contracted form of از که 'that/which,' introducing a subordinate clause. خفت و خیز is a paired noun phrase ('sleep and rising' — i.e. lying down and getting up/sexual relations) with زنان as its modifier ('with women').
segments.s482-g50: جوان پیر گردد is 'a young (person) becomes old' — گردد is the literary third-person present/subjunctive of 'become' used here as a general/future sense. به تن is a locative/affected phrase 'in (the) body' (به + noun), and بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. The overall clause says that youth turns to old age bodily.
segments.s482-g51: برآنم is a fixed idiom 'I am resolved / I intend.' که introduces the content clause that follows ('that you were sleepless for three nights'). بی‌خواب is an adjective 'sleepless' modifying the implied subject 'you,' and بودی is the past second-person form 'you were.' شب here is a count noun ('night') and combines with سه to mean 'three nights.'
segments.s482-g52: به من is the prepositional object 'to me' (به + pronoun). بازگوی is an imperative (or hortative) 'tell / recount' addressing the addressee; این is the demonstrative 'this/the foregoing.' و links the verbs; بگشای is an imperative 'open' used idiomatically with لب ('lip') as 'open your lips / speak,' i.e. 'speak up.'
segments.s482-g53: سکندر بدو گفت is again 'Alexander said to him' (name + indirect object + past verb). من روشنم is a first-person predicate 'I am روشنم' where روشنم is an adjectival predicate meaning 'clear/bright/well' in this context 'I am well / not troubled.' Note that گفت's usual sense 'said' is not present in the provided sense list below, so it was marked -1.
segments.s482-g54: از آزار is a prepositional phrase 'from/by suffering (harm).' سستی ندارد is a negative present construction 'does not have weakness' (ندارد = 'does not have'). تنم here is 'my body' (the possessive suffix -م), so the clause means 'my body does not have weakness from suffering,' i.e. 'I am not weakened by pain.' The lemma/senses given for تنم do not include 'body,' so it is marked -1.
segments.s482-g55: پسندیده دانای هندوستان is a noun phrase 'the پسندیده (esteemed/chosen) دانای (wise one) of هندوستان (India).' پسندیده here is adjectival/nominative meaning 'esteemed/selected,' modifying دانای 'the wise man/sage.'
segments.s482-g56: نبود is the past negative 'was not.' اندر آن کار is a locative-possessive phrase 'in that affair/matter' (اندر = in; آن = that; کار = affair/matter). همداستان is an adjective meaning 'of the same mind' or 'agreeing,' so the clause states that the Indian sage did not share the same opinion in that matter.
segments.s482-g25: گر is the poetic conditional particle 'if'. آری here is an emphatic particle meaning 'indeed' (not a simple answer). تو is the 2nd-person subject. این نغز دارو is a noun phrase with the adjective نغز ('delightful/fine') before the noun دارو ('remedy/medicine'); the whole phrase names the role/thing the addressee is being likened to. به جای is a prepositional phrase 'in the place of / instead of' and links this clause to the following clause (it sets the role the addressee would fill).
segments.s482-g26: تو باشی uses the present-subjunctive/optative (باشی) with the pronoun to make a polite, jussive request 'be you' / 'may you be' — here functioning like 'be (you)'. به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (گیتی = world). مرا را (written here as م + را) is the object 'me' with the definite/object marker را (م is the clitic for 'me' split in the line). رهنمای is a predicative noun 'guide' that the copula باشی takes: literally 'be (my) guide' with مرا/را marking the person affected.
segments.s482-g27: خریدار گردم is literally 'I become (your) buyer' and functions as 'I will buy you' or 'I will take you (as mine)'; گردم (from گشتن) is a 1st-person present/subjunctive form used here with an assertive/future force. ت + را is the object 'you' (ت second‑person clitic) plus the object marker را. من is an emphatic subject pronoun 'I'. به جان is an idiomatic instrumental phrase 'with (my) life' meaning 'at the cost of my life'.
segments.s482-g28: شوی is 2nd-person singular subjunctive of شدن used predictively/optatively here ('you shall be' / 'you will become'). بی‌گزند is a predicative adjective 'unharmed, safe'. از introduces the source or cause 'from / by' and is followed by بد بدگمان, literally 'evil (or bad) [of the] ill‑suspecting' — i.e. harm coming from malicious or distrustful people; syntactically this phrase qualifies the cause from which the addressee will be safe.
segments.s482-g29: ورا is an indirect‑object clitic/word meaning 'to him' or 'upon him'. خلعت is 'a robe (of honour)' and و connects it to نیکویها, 'good/fine things' (an irregular plural form meaning 'many fine things'). بساخت here is the past tense (3rd person) verb from ساختن with the prefix ب- (poetic) meaning 'he made / he bestowed' — it governs the preceding objects as the thing given to 'him'.
segments.s482-g30: ز marks the agent or source 'by / from' (poetic usage). دانا پزشکان is the agent 'the learned/wise physicians' (دانا qualifies پزشکان). سرش is the possessed noun 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش = his). برفراخت is a past tense verb meaning 'raised / uplifted / set aloft' (here passive/causative sense: 'his head was raised' or 'they raised his head'). Together: 'by the wise physicians his head was raised.'
segments.s482-g31: پزشک سراینده is a compound noun phrase: 'the physician who is also a composer/poet' (سراینده = one who composes/sings). آمد is the simple past 'came'. به کوه is a directional phrase 'to the mountain' (به + noun). The clause states movement: 'the physician-poet came to the mountain.'
segments.s482-g32: بیاورد is a subjunctive/purpose-form of 'bring' (بیاورد) used to express purpose after آمد: 'he came ... to bring.' با خویشتن means 'with himself' (i.e. accompanying him). زان is a demonstrative 'from that / of that' (here 'that' referring back to a previously mentioned company), and گروه is 'group/band'. The whole phrase expresses the purpose: 'to bring that group with him.'
segments.s482-g33: ز here introduces cause (’because of/through’); دانایی is the noun 'wisdom' and comes after ز as its object. او را فزون بود is an inverted, somewhat archaic order: literally 'to him was abundance' = 'he had abundant (wisdom)'; را marks the person as the beneficiary/topic of that state. بهر at the end is a linking preposition/adverb ('for/therefore') preparing the following clause.
segments.s482-g34: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle used before the verb; بشناخت is the simple past ('recognized/was able to recognize'). زهر is the direct object 'poison'. از پای زهر is an ablative phrase 'from the foot/base of the poison' — idiomatically 'from its slightest trace' — with از marking origin and پای used figuratively as 'trace/base'. The whole clause gives the consequence introduced by بهر above.
segments.s482-g35: گیاهان کوهی is a noun + adjectival form 'mountain plants/herbs' (noun before its qualifier); فراوان is a postpositive adjective 'abundant/plentiful'. درود is a poetic noun placed here after the phrase — in context it functions as an appositive/poetic tag attached to the herbs (literal sense 'greeting/blessing' is the dictionary sense given, used here in a lyrical way to describe the gathered plants).
segments.s482-g36: بیفگند is past tense 'he threw/scattered/dispensed'. The following ز و together represent 'از او' (from it/from them) in compressed poetic spelling; هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever', and بیکار بود is a predicate 'was useless/idle' (بود = was). Together: 'he threw away from them whatever was useless.'
segments.s482-g37: ازو is 'from it/from them' (prepositional phrase). پاک is the adjective 'pure' modifying تریاکها ('the opiates/opium drugs', plural). برگزید is the past tense verb 'he selected/chose' taking پاک تریاکها as its object.
segments.s482-g38: بیامیخت is past 'he mixed/combined' and takes دارو ('medicine/remedy') as its object. چنانچون introduces a comparative/qualifying clause 'in the way that/just as', and سزید here means 'was fitting/appropriate' (so the phrase says he mixed the medicines in the manner that was proper).
segments.s482-g39: تنش را = 'his body' with تن (body) + possessive clitic ش (his) and را as the definite/object marker. به داروی کوهی is an instrumental/agent phrase 'with/by (a) mountain remedy' (به + دارو + کوهی), and بشست is the past verb 'he washed'. The clause means 'he washed his body with mountain medicine/remedies.'
segments.s482-g40: همی introduces a continuing/resultant state; داشتش is a past/stative verb 'he kept it/was maintaining it' (داشت + clitic ش referring back to 'body'); سالیان is a temporal noun 'for years'; تن درست is a short predicate 'body [was] sound/healthy' with درست used in the sense 'proper/sound'. Altogether: 'he thereby kept his body healthy for years.'
segments.s482-g81: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. برزد is an archaic past verb here meaning 'rose/appeared' (poetic use of رزیدن). سر 'head, summit' is the thing that 'rose' and is followed by از + noun to mark the source ('rose from'). The phrase کوه روشن چراغ is a compact noun phrase 'the bright mountain's lamp' (poetic picture of the rising sun); word order is compressed and adjectival روشن follows the noun it modifies in classical style.
segments.s482-g82: چو again introduces a parallel temporal clause 'when'. دریا is the noun 'sea' (used image-wise here) and فروزنده is an adjective/participle 'radiant/illuminating'; شد is the past auxiliary 'became'. دشت و راغ are the coordinated nominative subjects (the plain and the rāgh) that are affected by that illumination. The clause uses inverted, poetic ordering but is equivalent to 'when the sea became radiant, the plain and the rāgh (were so).'
segments.s482-g83: سکندر is the proper subject 'Alexander'. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'to/onto that' (pointing back to the mentioned place) and بارگاه is the destination 'palace' or 'court'. The clause is straightforward S–V–(adverbial) in poetic diction.
segments.s482-g84: This is a descriptive verbless clause appended to Sکندر: 'دو لب' = 'two lips' (literally 'two lips'), followed by the predicate 'پر ز خنده' where پر = 'full (of)' takes the genitive introduced by ز (poetic 'of/from') and خنده = 'laughter'. The second half 'دل از غم تباه' likewise is predicate: دل 'heart' + از غم 'by/with sorrow' + تباه 'ruined/corrupt' (predicative adjective). The whole phrase describes Alexander's outward smile and inward ruined heart.
segments.s482-g85: فرستاده را is the direct object marked by the accusative particle را ('the envoy'). دید is the past tense verb 'saw' placed before the agent in this poetic order. سالار is the agent 'the commander' (subject). بار here is uncertain in exact nuance in the manuscript (likely a poetic or titular element attached to سالار), so its precise sense is not recoverable from the immediate clause.
segments.s482-g86: بپرسید is the past tense 'he asked/questioned' (3rd sing.). و = 'and' links the next action. بردش is the verb plus object-pronoun (برد + -ش) meaning 'he took/brought him'; the supplied sense list did not include this transitive meaning, so the exact listed sense does not match. بر here marks direction 'to/toward' (toward the king), and شهریار is the indirect object/goal 'the king'. Overall: 'he questioned him and took him to the king.'
segments.s482-g87: یکی is the indefinite 'one' or 'a certain'. بدره is a poetic epithet (literally 'full moon') used here adjectivally to mean 'beautiful/handsome' modifying دینار 'dinar, coin'. و = 'and' joins the coordinated noun اسپی 'steed/horse' modified by سیاه 'black'. The phrase lists the items to be given.
segments.s482-g88: بهرای is a prepositional/benefactive phrase 'for the sake of' or 'as (a reward) for'. زرین is the adjective 'golden' describing the nature of the reward. بفرمود is the formal/honorific past verb 'he commanded/ordained' (3rd sing.), and شاه 'the king' is the subject placed after the verb in elevated style. The clause means the king commanded those gifts as a golden reward.
segments.s482-g41: چنان شد که = a common classical formula meaning “it came to be that / so it happened that.” او شب نخفتی بسی places the actor (او) before the time (شب) and the past-negative verb نخفتی (did not sleep) — نخفتی is the past form with the negative prefix ن-. بسی is an adverb meaning “much”/“a great deal” and modifies نخفتی (i.e. ‘slept little’).
segments.s482-g42: بیامیختی here is the verb meaning ‘mixed/associated’ (classical form); in the edition the lemma/senses given do not match this usage, so it is treated as a verb ‘he mingled.’ شاد is a predicate adjective ‘merry/happy.’ با هر کسی is a prepositional phrase ‘with everyone,’ where هر کسی = ‘anyone/everyone’ and links as the object of ‘mingled.’
segments.s482-g43: به کار زنان = a prepositional phrase ‘in the affairs/matters of women’ (به + کار + زنان). تیز بودی is the past predicate ‘was sharp/keen’ (تیز = keen/sharp, بودی = past ‘was’). سرش (‘his head’/‘his mind’) is the logical subject placed after the predicate for poetic inversion; سر here conveys ‘mind/inner disposition.’
segments.s482-g44: همی is the archaic/progressive particle marking habitual or continuous action (here ‘would’/‘did’ regularly). نرم = ‘soft/gentle(ly)’ modifies the verb بجستی (past ‘sought’); جایی = ‘a place’ (somewhere). بجستی is the past form ‘he sought’ (or ‘he would seek’ in habitual sense). برش is the enclitic combination of بر + ش meaning ‘upon/next to her/him’ (not the lexical noun ‘cut’), i.e. it indicates direction/contact with the person.
segments.s482-g45: ازان سوی = ‘from that side / on the other hand’ (a linking adverbial). کاهش is a noun meaning ‘decline/wasting’ and is the endpoint toward which گرایید (past ‘turned / inclined’) — گرایید is the past tense of گراییدن ‘to turn/tend toward.’ شاه is the subject ‘the king.’ Word order is slightly inverted for emphasis: adverbial + noun + verb + subject.
segments.s482-g46: نکرد ... نگاه is a negative construction where نکرد (did not do) combines with the action noun نگاه (‘a look/glance’) to mean ‘did not look.’ اندر آن = ‘at/in that’ modifies تن (‘body’/‘person’), and را is the definite object marker attached to تن. هیچ intensifies the negation (‘not any / at all’). The verb (نکرد) precedes the verbal noun (نگاه) here for poetic order but together mean ‘did not cast any glance.’
segments.s482-g47: چنان بد که = ‘so bad that’ (cause/result connector). روزی in classical Persian can function adverbially as ‘one day’/‘one time’ here; بیامد is the past ‘came’ and پزشک is the subject ‘the physician.’ The clause is a simple past narrative: ‘one day a physician came.’
segments.s482-g48: ز کاهش = ‘from/because of wasting’ (ز = from/because of). نشان یافت is a compound: نشان = ‘sign’ and یافت = past ‘found’ → ‘found a sign.’ اندر سرشک = ‘in/within the tear’ (سرشک = a tear / small bead of liquid). The whole segment means the physician found a sign of wasting in the patient’s tear.
segments.s482-g73: سکندر بخندید is a simple past (perfective) clause: subject (سکندر) + verb (بخندید, 3rd person past). The conjunction و links the clauses; ز here is the poetic preposition ز = از (‘from/because of’), followed by a reduced pronoun (written here separately) giving زو = ‘from/through it’, and شاد شد is past tense 'became glad' (شد = become).
segments.s482-g74: ز تیمار وز درد آزاد شد uses repeatedز to mark separation from something: ز تیمار = 'from care' and (poetically contracted) وز درد = 'and from pain' (وز here represents و + ز in the verse). آزاد شد is past passive/raising meaning 'became free' or 'was freed'. The word order places the ablative-prepositional phrases before the predicate.
segments.s482-g75: وزان پس is the archaic/poetic equivalent of از آن پس ('from that time, thereafter'). ز داننده is an ablative/causal use = 'by/from the knower' (داننده = 'one who knows'); دل کرد شاد is a causative construction: دل (object/affected) + کرد (caused/made) + شاد (predicate adjective) = 'made his heart glad'.
segments.s482-g76: ورا گفت = 'he said to him' where ورا is the dative 'to him' and گفت is past 'said' (note: the provided lemma senses list treats گفت as a noun; here it is the verb گفتِ (to say) in past). بی‌هند is the compound with بی- meaning 'without' (here 'without Hind/India'); گیتی مباد uses مباد (optative/negative jussive auxiliary) meaning 'may it not be' — a conventional wish formula: 'may the world not be without Hind.'
segments.s482-g77: بزرگان و اخترشناسان همه lists two groups in apposition: 'the great ones' and 'the astrologers/astronomers', followed by همه 'all' (collective emphasis). Word order is noun + و + noun + postpositive همه.
segments.s482-g78: تو گویی is an idiomatic parenthetical meaning 'as if one would say' or 'it seemed'; تو is literal 'you' used in this formulaic expression and گویی (here the verbal form 'گوئی') functions like 'it seems / one would say'. به هندوستان شد رمه places the direction/goal before the predicate: به هندوستان 'to India', شد 'became/went' (past), and رمه 'a flock' (metaphorical collective), i.e. 'they seemed to have turned into a flock bound for India.'
segments.s482-g79: وزانجا is the fused poetic locative 'وز آن جا' = 'from there'; بیامد is past 'he came'. سوی خان خویش is a directional phrase 'toward his khan/liege' where خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'own' and the enclitic ش marks 'his' (خویش‌ش = 'his own'). Word order: adverbial + verb + prepositional direction + possessor phrase.
segments.s482-g80: همه شب 'all night' (= throughout the night) + همی (archaic continuous/frequentative particle) marks ongoing action; ساخت is past 'made/prepared' and درمان خویش is the object phrase 'his remedy' (درمان = remedy/treatment, خویش + ش = his own). The clause thus means 'he spent the whole night preparing his remedy.'
segments.s482-g57: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'. شب تیره شد is a simple past-state clause: شب (night) + تیره (dark) + شد (became) = 'the night became dark.' آن نبشته is the object 'that writing/inscription' and بجست is a past-tense transitive verb ('he sought'); the verb follows its object here in a typical archaic/poetic order.
segments.s482-g58: بیاورد is a past narrative form meaning 'he brought' (classical/past third-person). داروی کاهش درست is a noun phrase: داروی (the medicine/remedy of) + کاهش (reduction/alleviation; here the target/aim of the remedy) + درست (proper/correct) — an attributive adjective modifying the remedy.
segments.s482-g59: همان نیز functions adverbially as 'likewise' or 'also'. تنها سکندر is the topicalized subject 'Alexander alone/only', and بخفت is an archaic past form meaning 'he lay down / slept' (ب‑ prefix appears in classical narrative forms).
segments.s482-g60: نیامیخت is a past negative verb meaning 'did not mingle/associate/join'. با introduces the accompanying phrase 'with', here با ماه ('with the moon' — i.e. in the moonlit context). دیدار جفت is a nominal phrase 'meeting of pairs/couples' (دیدار = meeting; جفت = pair), so the whole clause says he did not join the moonlit meetings of couples.
segments.s482-g61: به ... اندر آمد is a classical locative/temporal frame 'at / into ... came'. شبگیر هور here marks the time/place ('at nightfall' or 'into Hoor at night' — هور can be a proper name or a place/epithet in epic contexts), and پزشک is the subject 'the physician' placed after the verb phrase, a common flexible word order in older Persian.
segments.s482-g62: نگه کرد is the light-verb construction from نگه‌داشتن/نگاه کردن meaning 'he looked' or 'he kept watch' (کرد is the light verb). و = 'and'. بی‌بار here modifies the following noun phrase and conveys 'single/without other burden' (i.e. a lone/unshared thing). دیدش is دید + ـش (clitic) = 'he saw him/it', and سرشک is 'a teardrop' — so 'he looked and saw a single teardrop on him.'
segments.s482-g63: بینداخت is a past transitive verb 'he threw/cast aside'. دارو is its object 'the medicine/remedy'. به رامش نشست is an intransitive clause: به رامش ('into tranquillity/peace') + نشست (past 'sat down' / 'took a seat') — so two coordinated actions: he cast aside the medicine and sat in repose.
segments.s482-g64: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one' / 'someone'. جام بگرفت is a simple past clause 'took a cup' (بگرفت = seized/took). شادان functions adverbially/adjectivally 'joyful' and به دست 'into/into his hand' describes how he held it — literally 'joyful in (his) hand' i.e. he seized the cup, joyous, holding it in his hand.
segments.s482-g65: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he ordered/said'; تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that'/'that'); خوان is the object 'the table/banquet'; بیاراستند is a past-plural verb ('they arranged/adorned'), so the clause literally reads 'he ordered that they set/arrange the banquet.'
segments.s482-g66: نوازنده رود is a nominal phrase 'the player/performer of the رود' (رود here = a musical string instrument in classical usage); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; می‌خواستند is past/habitual 'they wanted' or 'they meant to/were about to,' describing the musicians' disposition or intention.
segments.s482-g67: بدو is the prepositional/clitic form 'to him' (introducing the indirect object); گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used with the indirect object phrase); شاه is the subject 'the king'; آن چرا ریختی is a direct question fragment: آن 'that', چرا 'why', ریختی the past second-person form 'you poured/put (in)'.
segments.s482-g68: چو here introduces a temporal/circumstantial clause 'when' (or 'as'); با = 'with'; رنج = 'suffering/pain'; دارو = 'medicine'; برآمیختی is a past second-person verb with the prefix بر- 'you mixed (together)' — together the clause says 'when you mixed the medicine with suffering.'
segments.s482-g69: ورا is 'to him' (alternative form of the indirect-object marker); گفت = 'said' (past); شاه جهان is a title phrase 'the king of the world' (شاه modified by جهان); دوش means 'last night' and جفت is the noun 'mate/spouse' (object of the following verb).
segments.s482-g70: نجست is a past verb here meaning 'he sought/searched for' (so 'جفت نجست' = 'sought a mate'); و = 'and'; شب تیره is a noun phrase 'dark night'; تنها بخفت contains the past verb بخفت 'he slept' with تنها 'alone' — 'he slept alone.'
segments.s482-g71: چو here is the conditional/temporal 'if/when'; تنها 'alone'; بخسپی is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person verbal form meaning 'you sleep' (variant of بخسبی); تو‌ای is the vocative 'O you', and شهریار is 'king' — together addressing the king: 'If you sleep alone, O king...'.
segments.s482-g72: نیاید is a negative impersonal/potential 'will not come' or 'does not come' used idiomatically with به کار آمدن to mean 'be of use'; ترا is the clitic object 'to you' (ت + را); هیچ 'any/none'; دارو 'medicine'; به کار 'to use' — the clause means 'no medicine will be of use to you.'
segments.s482-g1: بفرمود is the 3rd-person past/honorific of فرمودن (“he said/ordered”); here it functions as ‘he commanded’. تا introduces a purpose/result clause (’so that/that’). رفت is the past 3rd-singular ‘went’. پیشش is پیش + enclitic pronoun meaning ‘to/into his presence’ (the indirect object). پزشک is the noun ‘physician/doctor’ as the person sent.
segments.s482-g2: که here introduces the content/purpose clause ‘that’. علت is the noun ‘cause’ (object to be explained). بگفتی is an old/poetic optative/subjunctive form meaning ‘(that) he should say / should tell’ (not a plain past here). چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. دیدی is the 2nd/3rd-person past of ‘see’ (here ‘when he sees’). سرشک is the object ‘teardrop/tear’. The clause thus reads ‘that he should tell the cause when he saw the tear’.
segments.s482-g3: سر دردمندی is a nominal phrase in which سر has the sense ‘chief/leader’ and دردمندی ‘the afflicted (person)’, so together ‘the chief/leader among the afflicted’ (the subject). بدو is the prepositional phrase ‘to him’. گفت is the 3rd-person past ‘said’ (reporting speech). چیست is the interrogative copula ‘what is it?’ (چی + است).
segments.s482-g4: که introduces an explanatory/content clause ‘that’. بر is the preposition ‘on/upon/for’. درد is ‘pain’ (here general suffering). زان پس is a temporal sequence: زان ‘from/that’ + پس ‘after’ = ‘after that/thereafter’. بباید is the impersonal/modal verb ‘one must/it is necessary’. گریست is the verb ‘to weep’ (infinitival/oblique use after بباید), so the clause reads ‘that for the pain one must afterwards weep.’
segments.s482-g5: بدو گفت is the matrix reporting ‘he said to him’. هر کس که introduces a generic relative clause ‘whoever/anyone who’. افزون modifies خورد and together means ‘eats in excess’ or ‘eats more than is proper’. خورد is the past 3rd-singular ‘ate’ used generically as a habitual/predicate verb.
segments.s482-g6: چو is the temporal ‘when’. بر خوان نشیند is a temporal phrase ‘when he sits at the table’ (بر = ‘at/on’, خوان = ‘banquet/table’, نشیند = 3rd-singular present/subjunctive ‘sits’). خورش ننگرد is a negative verbal clause: خورش ‘the dish/food’ is the object, ننگرد is the negative present/subjunctive/indicative of نگرستن ‘to look/regard’, here ‘he does not look at / does not mind the food’. The negative prefix ن- attaches to the verb.
segments.s482-g7: نباشد is a classical negative copular/existential form meaning ‘there is not / it is not the case that’. فراوان خورش is the noun phrase ‘plentiful food/dish’. تن درست is a nominal phrase ‘a sound/healthy body’ (تن = body, درست = sound/proper). The whole phrase expresses that abundant food is not what makes a body healthy.
segments.s482-g8: بزرگ is the adjective ‘great’. آنک is the relative pronoun ‘who/that’ linking to the omitted antecedent (‘he’/‘one’). او is the pronoun ‘he’. تن درستی is the object phrase ‘bodily soundness/health’ (درستی here = ‘soundness/health’). بجست is the past 3rd-singular of جستن ‘to seek’ (‘sought’). The clause therefore reads ‘great is he who sought bodily health.’
segments.s482-g9: بیامیزم is a first-person present-subjunctive/jussive form ('let me / I will mix'); here it expresses the speaker's intention. اکنون is an adverb ('now') placed after the verb. ترا + را are clitic elements marking the addressee and the definite object (ترا = 'for you / you' as a clitic; را = the object marker attached to the following noun), and دارویی is the definite noun 'a remedy' that is the object of بیامیزم.
segments.s482-g10: گیاها is a collective noun 'herbs/plants' serving as the material source. فراز here functions adverbially ('on high / from above / from afar'). آرم is first-person singular (here presenting the speaker's action 'I bring / fetch' in older usage). از هر سویی is a prepositional phrase ('from every side/direction') specifying origin.
segments.s482-g11: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'). همواره باشی is a subjunctive construction ('may you always be / may you always remain'); باشی is the subjunctive/optative form. تو is the (emphatic) pronoun 'you'. زان تن درست is a short demonstrative + noun phrase ('that sound/proper body'), with زان = 'that (of/from that)' modifying تن درست.
segments.s482-g12: نباید is an impersonal/modal negator ('one must not / it should not'); it governs the following verb phrase. به here functions instrumentally ('with / by means of'), دارو is the instrument 'the medicine', and ترا+را again mark the second-person clitic and object marker attached in the phrase. دست شست is a verbal phrase where شست is the past stem 'washed' used after نباید to express prohibition ('must not wash (one's) hand(s)').
segments.s482-g13: همان is a demonstrative 'the same / that very', modifying آرزوها ('wishes'). بیفزایدت is a third-person singular present/subjunctive verb 'may (they) add/increase' with the enclitic ت marking the beneficiary/object 'you' (i.e. 'may those wishes increase you'). Word order puts subject (آرزوها) before the verb.
segments.s482-g14: چو is a conditional/subordinating particle here ('if/when'). افزون qualifies خوری (خوری = 'you eat') to mean 'eat more / overeat'. چیز is the preverbal object/emphatic noun 'thing' or 'anything', and نگزایدت is a negative verbal form (ن‑ prefix + گزاید) with the enclitic ت ('you') meaning 'will not harm/afflict you'. The clause thus reads as a conditional: 'if you eat in excess, nothing will harm you' (interpretation depends on context; grammatically, چو + verb ... + ن-verb expresses the conditional-result relationship).
segments.s482-g15: همان یاد is 'that same memory/remembrance' (demonstrative + noun). داری is the present second-person singular of داشتن ('you have/you keep'), so the phrase means 'you keep that remembrance' or 'retain that memory'. سخنهای نغز is a following noun phrase 'pithy/delightful words' (سخنهای = plural 'words/sayings', نغز = 'pithy/witty/delicious' in a figurative sense).
segments.s482-g16: بیفزاید is a third-person singular present verb 'it/they will add or increase' whose subject is the preceding سخنهای نغز. اندر تنت is a locative phrase 'into/within your body' (اندر = 'in/within'; تنت = تن + ت = 'your body'). خون و مغز are the direct objects 'blood and marrow/brain' that are said to be increased into the body by those words.

sentences.s482-s45: He gave it to the wise physician and said, "May wisdom be paired with the pure standard."
sentences.s482-s9: May you become content in your own person; your heart will grow glad like the joyful spring.
sentences.s482-s10: May that same hue appear on your face; may you gain good counsel in every pure deed.
sentences.s482-s11: May your scattered hair not turn white; let not the world's whiteness make you despair.
sentences.s482-s12: Alexander said to him, 'I have not heard (of this); I have seen no one among kings like this.'
sentences.s482-s25: He said to him that by sleeping and rising with women a young man will surely grow old in his body.
sentences.s482-s26: I am determined that you were sleepless for three nights; tell me this and open your lips.
sentences.s482-s27: Alexander said to him, 'I am well; my body is not weakened by suffering.'
sentences.s482-s28: The esteemed wise man of India was not of the same mind in that matter.
sentences.s482-s13: If indeed you are this fine remedy in my stead, be my guide in the world.
sentences.s482-s14: I will purchase you with my life; you will be unhurt by evil and by those who are ill‑disposed.
sentences.s482-s15: He bestowed upon him a robe and many fine things.
sentences.s482-s16: A physician-poet came to the mountain to bring that band with him.
sentences.s482-s17: Because he was especially wise, he could even recognize poison from its slightest trace.
sentences.s482-s18: He spread plentiful mountain herbs, and from them he cast away whatever was useless.
sentences.s482-s19: From them he chose the pure opiates and mixed a medicine as was fitting.
sentences.s482-s20: He washed his body with mountain remedies; thus he kept his body sound for years.
sentences.s482-s41: When the lamp's head rose above the bright mountain, and when the sea grew radiant, the plain and the rāgh were illuminated.
sentences.s482-s42: Alexander came to that palace, his two lips full of laughter and his heart ruined by sorrow.
sentences.s482-s43: The commander saw the envoy, questioned him, and brought him before the king.
sentences.s482-s44: The king ordered (that) one handsome dinar and a black steed as a golden reward.
sentences.s482-s21: It so happened that he slept little at night and merrily mingled with everyone.
sentences.s482-s22: He was keen in matters of women; softly he sought a place beside her.
sentences.s482-s23: On the other hand the king inclined toward wasting; he did not cast any glance at that body.
sentences.s482-s24: It became so bad that one day a physician came and found a sign of wasting in a tear.
sentences.s482-s37: Alexander laughed, and because of that he became glad; he was freed from care and from pain.
sentences.s482-s38: From that time his heart was made glad by the knower, and he said to him, 'May the world not be without Hind.'
sentences.s482-s39: All the great men and the astrologers — it seemed — had become a flock heading for India.
sentences.s482-s40: From there he came to his khan; all night long he kept preparing his cure.
sentences.s482-s29: When the night grew dark he sought that writing and brought the proper remedy to lessen it.
sentences.s482-s30: Alexander likewise lay down alone; he did not join the moonlit meetings of couples.
sentences.s482-s31: At nightfall the physician entered (into Hoor's chamber); he gazed, and saw a single teardrop on him.
sentences.s482-s32: He threw away the medicine and sat down in repose; one of them seized a cup, joyful in hand.
sentences.s482-s33: He commanded that the banquet be set; the musician played the lute and they were eager (to perform).
sentences.s482-s34: The king said to him, 'Why did you pour that, when you mixed the drug with suffering?'
sentences.s482-s35: The king said to him: 'Last night he sought a mate, and in the dark night he slept alone.'
sentences.s482-s36: If you sleep alone, O king, no medicine will be of any use to you.
sentences.s482-s1: He ordered a physician to go to him so that he would explain the cause when he saw the teardrop.
sentences.s482-s2: The chief of the afflicted said to him, ‘What is it?’ — that because of the pain one must afterwards weep.
sentences.s482-s3: He told him: ‘Whoever eats in excess, when he sits at the table pays no heed to his food.’
sentences.s482-s4: Plentiful food will not make the body sound; great is he who sought bodily health.
sentences.s482-s5: I will now mix for you a herbal remedy, fetching the herbs from every direction.
sentences.s482-s6: So that you may always remain sound in that body, you must not wash your hands with the medicine.
sentences.s482-s7: May those very wishes increase you; if you overeat, nothing will harm you.
sentences.s482-s8: Keep that remembrance; pithy words will add blood and marrow to your body.
